attributes.stem:
A 36-year-old woman is admitted to hospital for an elective Caesarean section at 39 weeks gestation. This is her third pregnancy, with two previous vaginal deliveries. She requests permanent contraception at the time of surgery as she is certain she does not want further children. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. Her family history is significant for ovarian cancer in her maternal aunt at age 45. The patient's BRCA genetic testing is negative.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The pregnancy is uncomplicated with a cephalic presentation.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate surgical approach for contraception?
attributes.explanation:
This patient requesting permanent contraception presents an opportunity to reduce her future ovarian cancer risk, particularly given her family history. Recent evidence suggests that many high-grade serous ovarian cancers originate in the fallopian tubes rather than the ovaries themselves.

Bilateral salpingectomy provides the greatest protection against future ovarian cancer by removing both fallopian tubes in their entirety. This procedure has been shown to reduce ovarian cancer risk by up to 65% compared to no surgery, while preserving normal ovarian function and hormone production.

Bilateral tubal ligation, while effective for contraception, provides less protection against ovarian cancer compared to complete removal of the tubes. The residual tubal tissue may still undergo malignant transformation.

Bilateral filschie clips are a form of mechanical occlusion that leaves the tubes intact. While effective for contraception, they provide minimal protection against future ovarian cancer as all tubal tissue remains.

Bilateral salpingoophorectomy would remove both tubes and ovaries, which is unnecessarily extensive in this case. This would result in surgical menopause requiring hormone replacement therapy and is not appropriate for a 36-year-old woman requesting contraception only.

Partial salpingectomy leaves behind portions of the fallopian tubes that could potentially undergo malignant transformation. The protective effect would be less than complete removal.

The timing of the Caesarean section provides an ideal opportunity for bilateral salpingectomy as it can be performed through the same incision without significantly increasing operative time or complications. This approach is increasingly being recommended as the preferred method of surgical sterilisation due to its protective effect against ovarian cancer.
learningPoints: Bilateral salpingectomy at the time of Caesarean section reduces the risk of subsequent ovarian cancer compared to tubal ligation.
Bilateral salpingectomy does not affect ovarian function or hormone levels.
High-grade serous ovarian cancer often originates in the fallopian tubes.
references: Berek JS, Renz M, Kehoe S, Kumar L, Friedlander M. Cancer of the ovary, fallopian tube, and peritoneum: 2021 update. Int J Gynaecol Obstet. 2021 Oct;155 Suppl 1(Suppl 1):61-85. doi: 10.1002/ijgo.13878. PMID: 34669199; PMCID: PMC9298325.

Kahn RM, Gordhandas S, Godwin K, Stone RL, Worley MJ Jr, Lu KH, Long Roche KC. Salpingectomy for the Primary Prevention of Ovarian Cancer: A Systematic Review. JAMA Surg. 2023 Nov 1;158(11):1204-1211. doi: 10.1001/jamasurg.2023.4164. Erratum in: JAMA Surg. 2023 Nov 1;158(11):1229. doi: 10.1001/jamasurg.2023.5347. Erratum in: JAMA Surg. 2023 Dec 1;158(12):1358. doi: 10.1001/jamasurg.2023.5756. PMID: 37672283; PMCID: PMC11185162.
